pair_convention.spot_explanation: USD/CAD at 1.3570 means 1 USD buys 1.3570 CAD. A rising USD/CAD rate means the USD is strengthening versus the CAD (CAD weakening). A falling USD/CAD rate means the CAD is strengthening versus the USD (USD weakening). This is an inverted convention from a CAD perspective — 'bullish CAD' means the pair FALLS. All targets and scenario levels are expressed as USD/CAD outright rates.

executive_summary: USD/CAD is trading at 1.3576 as of March 11, 2026, having ground lower from the October 2025 high of 1.4791 — a 15% CAD appreciation over five months. The bilateral narrative is a genuine tug-of-war: the USD dossier points to structural weakness from a stagflationary data mix (NFP -92K, core PCE 3.0%), a closing growth gap, and twin deficits at 8.8% of GDP, while the CAD dossier features a contracted Q4 GDP (-0.6% annualised), a services PMI stuck in contraction at 46.5, and the BoC-Fed rate differential of -138bps that mechanically bleeds long-CAD positions 48 pips per quarter. The dominant bilateral narrative is rate convergence: the Fed is expected to cut at least once by September while the BoC holds at 2.25%, mechanically compressing the -138bps spread — the structural engine that drives the medium-term CAD appreciation thesis. What neither standalone dossier fully captures is the pair-specific dynamic from the Iran/Hormuz oil shock: Canada benefits asymmetrically relative to the US, receiving a positive terms-of-trade shock (WCS-WTI compression to $12/bbl, TMX at 890K bbl/d) without the safe-haven demand that has propped up DXY. Tactically, the pair is range-bound as the deeply oversold RSI (20.1) argues for a technical bounce toward 1.37 before the March 16–19 super-week resolves the direction. Strategically, we are bearish USD/CAD (bullish CAD) at conviction 3/5, targeting 1.30–1.33 by August 2026, conditioned on constructive USMCA development and oil remaining above $70/bbl.

market_context.valuation_assessment: The USD dossier pegs USD/CAD PPP fair value at approximately 1.23, implying 11% USD overvaluation at 1.357. The CAD dossier corroborates: USD/CAD is approximately 10–12% above PPP, with the BoC CERI at ~96 (35th historical percentile, mildly undervalued). The bilateral PPP deviation of 10–12% reflects three compounding factors: the cumulative carry bleed of -138bps favouring USD/CAD higher, the USMCA risk premium (~3–5% structural overhang), and the growth differential (US GDP 2.2% annualised in Q3 vs Canada -0.6% in Q4). Bank consensus at 1.33–1.34 year-end implies the market prices approximately half the PPP deviation as structural (persistent) and half as cyclical (to be unwound). RBC sees USD/CAD drifting to 1.3400 by end-2026, conditional on the BoC shifting to hikes, the Fed on hold or cutting rates, and most Canadian exports entering the US duty-free.

market_context.recent_regime: USD/CAD has been in a five-month downtrend dominated by three overlapping dynamics: (1) the BoC completing its 275bp easing cycle and pivoting to two-way guidance, which removed the primary catalyst for further CAD weakness; (2) the Iran/Hormuz oil shock from February 28 creating asymmetric benefits for Canada's non-Hormuz export routes via TMX Pacific access; and (3) the unwinding of the tariff risk premium as the Supreme Court struck down IEEPA tariffs in February 2026, with the US House subsequently voting to repeal tariffs imposed on Canadian goods. The current tension is between a technically oversold pair (RSI 20.1) with two failed weekly closes above 1.3725 resistance and a super-week event cluster (seven central bank decisions in three days) that could resolve the coiling pattern forcefully in either direction.

bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The bilateral carry on USD/CAD is the dominant tactical headwind for long-CAD positions. With Fed Funds at 3.625% and the BoC Overnight Rate at 2.25%, the annualised carry cost of holding long CAD/short USD is -138bps. At a USD/CAD rate of approximately 1.35, the carry drag translates to approximately 47–48 pips per quarter. The negative carry on CAD means USD/CAD forwards price ABOVE spot — the forward curve implies mild CAD depreciation at each tenor (1M +11 pips, 3M +34 pips, 6M +68 pips). Carry is working AGAINST the bullish CAD thesis at the tactical horizon: the -138bps annualised drag means the pair must appreciate ~3.6% over 6 months just to justify a carry-neutral long position, explaining why leveraged funds are structurally short CAD despite the fundamental picture. The expected path of Fed cuts (first cut September, -25bps) narrows the differential from -138bps to approximately -113bps, improving the carry picture but not reversing it. The Scotiabank outlier scenario of BoC hikes (+50bps H2 2026) would narrow the differential to ~-88bps — potentially a tipping point for carry traders to reduce shorts.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral rate differential is the most important factor for USD/CAD at both horizons. The Fed Funds rate at 3.625% versus the BoC at 2.25% establishes a -138bps differential that is the single largest persistent headwind for CAD appreciation. This is wider than it has been for most of the past decade and is the primary reason leveraged funds hold net-short CAD positions. Both central banks are currently on hold: FOMC at 92–97% hold probability for March 18, BoC at 94% hold for March 18. The critical near-term dynamic is the simultaneous 24-hour window on March 17–18 where the FOMC releases its dot plot on March 18 and the BoC announces on the same day — a paired binary event unlike anything in recent FX history. The CGB-UST 2Y spread sits at -93bps, reflecting the policy differential, while the 10Y spread of -75bps has been narrowing as the market begins pricing Fed cuts. Tactically, the carry differential is static and stable — no catalyst moves it within 2-4 weeks unless the dot plot surprises hawkish (widens) or the BoC surprises hawkish (narrows).

tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices static differentials through the tactical horizon, consistent with both banks on hold. The FOMC dot plot is the live variable — a hawkish surprise (zero 2026 cuts) would widen the spread and push USD/CAD higher by 50–80 pips; a dovish surprise (2+ cuts) would narrow it and push the pair below 1.35.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The BoC ran ahead of the Fed on the rate-cutting floor and has been left with little room to move further on interest rates, while depressed Canadian interest rates mean Loonie flows on rate differentials have only one direction to go as the Fed looks more likely to step up its pace of rate cuts. The structural argument for USD/CAD falling is straightforward: the Fed is expected to cut to 3.25–3.50% by September while the BoC holds (or possibly hikes via the Scotiabank scenario), compressing the bilateral spread from -138bps toward -100bps or narrower. RBC's rates strategists highlight that the BoC is likely to hold rates at the bottom-end of its neutral range of 2.25–3.25% this year. This rate convergence is the primary structural driver for the 6-month bearish USD/CAD view. The April 29 BoC MPR is the key asymmetric event: if the BoC raises its 2026 GDP forecast above 1.5%, the market would begin pricing BoC hikes, potentially collapsing the differential to -50bps or less — a powerful CAD tailwind not in any bank's base case. The UST-CGB 10Y spread is expected to narrow from +75bps toward +50bps as Fed cuts materialise.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical neutral and strategic bearish USD/CAD differ because: (1) the static carry cost dominates at the tactical horizon without an event to override it; (2) the March 18 simultaneous FOMC + BoC creates symmetric two-way risk that justifies neutral positioning; and (3) over 6 months, the probability-weighted path of Fed cuts compressing the spread makes the directional signal clearer. The inflection is March 18 — if the dot plot confirms one cut, strategic bearish conviction is maintained; if dots show zero cuts, the strategic timeline extends but the direction is unchanged.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral growth picture at the tactical horizon is nuanced and supports mild USD/CAD weakness (CAD strengthening) primarily because US growth is surprising to the downside more visibly than Canadian growth. The US posted NFP of -92K (3rd contraction in 5 months) while Canada posted Q4 GDP of -0.6% — both weak, but the US stagflationary signal (NFP -92K alongside ISM Prices Paid at 70.5 and core PCE at 3.0%) is more unambiguous and more directly bearish for the dollar. Canada's weakness is partially offset by the manufacturing PMI crossing above 50 for two consecutive months and the Ivey PMI surging to 56.6. The upcoming February CPI releases for both countries are critical: US February CPI today; Canada February CPI expected March 16 with a likely spike toward 2.9% on gasoline. If the US CPI is hot and Canada's is also hot, both currencies face conflicting signals but the Fed paralysis narrative (stagflation) is more negative for USD than a hot Canadian CPI is positive for BoC hike expectations. Crucially, the US data narrative has deteriorated faster than Canada's: the S&P Global Composite PMI differential, which had averaged +3 points in favour of the US for two years, has now closed to zero.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic bilateral growth picture is genuinely uncertain. The US growth trajectory is heading lower (GDPNow revised from 3.1% to 2.1%, three negative NFP prints in five months), but Canada is growing at a potentially lower trend rate (~0–1% potential) with Q4 contraction. The issue is which deteriorates faster: if US labour market weakness accelerates and forces Fed to cut more aggressively than priced, this is CAD-positive; if Canada enters a technical recession (back-to-back negative quarters), the BoC would reconsider cuts, widening the differential and hurting CAD. Canada's growth asymmetry in the Scotiabank scenario (BoC hikes) requires the economy to stabilise above 1% — the April 29 MPR is the definitive update. Among IMF main reserve-currency economies, Canada stands out as the country with the largest net energy balance relative to the size of its economy, equivalent to a hefty 4.4% of nominal GDP — this energy income provides a structural growth buffer that the US lacks in the current oil-price environment. However, per-capita GDP remains deeply negative in Canada.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The risk environment's impact on USD/CAD is muted relative to higher-beta currency pairs (AUD/JPY, NZD/JPY) because CAD occupies a unique bilateral position: it is simultaneously a commodity currency (risk-on when oil rises) and a US-adjacent economy (partially safe-haven via deep US integration). The Iran/Hormuz crisis has created an unusual situation where the standard risk-off playbook has been partially inverted for USD/CAD: USD is supported by safe-haven demand (bearish for most non-USD currencies), but oil's surge is simultaneously positive for CAD. These forces largely cancel. VIX at 22.8–24.3 (~60th–65th percentile) is elevated but not crisis-level — not sufficient to generate the kind of systematic flight to USD that would overwhelm oil's CAD tailwind. CAD's beta to the S&P 500 is approximately 0.3–0.4, materially lower than AUD (0.6) or NZD (0.7). The TSX Composite's massive 1-year return of +38.1% (led by energy and materials) indicates that Canadian risk assets are benefiting from the exact environment hurting US equities.

The more important strategic risk factor for USD/CAD is USMCA: a constructive review outcome removes 3–5% risk premium (bearish USD/CAD); a confrontational outcome adds 3–5% (bullish USD/CAD). Canada's deep integration with the US financial system and TSX outperformance suggest that CAD is not being treated as a traditional 'risk-off' currency in the current environment — it is being treated as an energy play. The strategic risk picture for the pair is therefore dominated by the oil channel and trade policy, not traditional VIX-based sentiment.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral positioning picture for USD/CAD creates a meaningful asymmetry favouring downside. The composite signal from combining both dossiers' CFTC data is that leveraged funds are short CAD (-41,371 contracts, added -7,270 in the latest week) while asset managers are long CAD (+61,582 contracts). In USD/CAD terms: leveraged funds are implicitly long USD/CAD (benefiting from pair rising) while real money is short USD/CAD (benefiting from pair falling). The majority of the leveraged fund short was built at USD/CAD levels above 1.38–1.39; at current 1.357, many positions are in drawdown of 200–300 pips, creating mechanical covering pressure around events that could accelerate the move. Large investors including Canadian pension funds appear to be unwinding short CAD positions and shifting toward net long exposure — the first meaningful shift in large investor CAD sentiment in nearly three years. The March 16–19 super-week is precisely the type of concentrated event cluster that forces leveraged funds to reduce risk. The contrarian signal is clear: at the 70th–75th percentile of leverage fund CAD bearishness, the crowded short creates a squeeze risk into any directional catalyst.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: At the strategic horizon, positioning signals fade as fundamentals reassert. The more important observation is the structural divergence: hedge fund shorts versus real-money longs. Over 3–6 months, real-money asset managers tend to be better directional forecasters, and their +61,582 long CAD position represents a structural allocation to the rate convergence + energy infrastructure thesis. Canadian bank expectations generally point to short-term consolidation, followed by gradual CAD appreciation later in 2026, assuming inflation remains contained and global risk sentiment stays stable. If the leveraged fund shorts cover over Q2, that generates cumulative buying pressure of potentially 200+ pips. The risk is that fresh shorts enter on any CAD weakness, extending the leveraged fund bearish position toward -60K contracts — the level at which the carry trade thesis would be re-asserting dominance.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral net commodity exposure calculation for USD/CAD with oil rising is unambiguously CAD-positive. Oil direction rules applied: Oil ↑ → ++CAD, mildly positive USD (US now net energy exporter). Net bilateral effect: CAD gets a strong positive (++), USD gets a mild positive (+). Net = pair falls (CAD appreciates, USD/CAD lower). Canada is the larger beneficiary of elevated oil for three reasons: (1) Canada's energy sector represents ~15% of GDP vs US at ~9%; (2) the TMX pipeline provides Pacific access at Brent-linked pricing, capturing more of the Iran premium than US inland crude; (3) the WCS-WTI spread has compressed to $12/bbl from $15.75, meaning Canadian heavy crude producers are capturing approximately $3.75/bbl more per barrel than the WTI headline implies. The bulk of the US trade deficit with Canada is owing to energy; outside of that, the scales tip into America's favour. The current account differential: US at -3.6% of GDP vs Canada at -1.0% of GDP (and likely improving in Q1 2026 as energy trade surplus expands). The twin deficit (US at 8.8% of GDP) overwhelms Canada's fiscal position (Canada federal + provincial deficit ~3.5% of GDP). Bilaterally, this creates a structural USD-negative, CAD-positive external balance backdrop.

tactical.what_is_priced: The oil-driven terms-of-trade improvement is partially reflected in USD/CAD's 5–7% decline over 6 months. The permanent structural improvement from TMX (WCS-WTI compression) is NOT fully priced in bank forecasts that use static oil assumptions.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic external balance comparison is one of the clearest bilateral differentials: Canada's energy export infrastructure has undergone a secular transformation that bank models have been slow to incorporate. Canada's trade advantage in energy has been rising steadily in recent years, most recently on the back of the Trans Mountain Pipeline Expansion (TMX) that has sharply boosted Canadian oil exports to the west coast in addition to Asian markets. The LNG Canada Phase 1 ramp to 14 MTPA adds a structurally positive natural gas export stream. The TMX Phase 2 optimisation to 1.25M bbl/d (DRA filing Q1 2026) would increase throughput ~10% without new construction. Meanwhile, the US external position continues to deteriorate: NIIP at -$27.6T (-88% of GDP), TIC selling by Japan, China, and Brazil, and COFER USD share at a 30-year low of 56.92%. The bilateral current account comparison (US at -3.6% vs Canada at -1.0%, trending toward balance on oil at $70+) creates structural demand for CAD vs supply for USD.

factors.horizon_split: Bullish CAD at both horizons. Tactical confidence equal (65 vs 65) because the oil shock is immediate and quantifiable (WCS-WTI compression, TMX utilisation data), while the strategic case adds the secular infrastructure argument that operates over years but is confirmed by current data.

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: USD: US is approximately net energy break-even; mild positive from oil exports (US net energy exporter since 2019) but substantially less leveraged to oil upside than Canada. Energy sector ~9% of GDP.
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: CAD: Canada is a major net oil and gas exporter. Energy sector ~15% of GDP. TMX provides Pacific access at Brent-linked pricing. LNG Canada Phase 1 at 14 MTPA ramping. WCS-WTI spread structural compression from $15.75 to $12/bbl.
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Step 1: Oil rises (Iran/Hormuz shock, WTI ~$81–83). Step 2: Apply direction rules — Oil ↑ → CAD strongly positive (++), USD mildly positive (+) as US is now a net exporter but less leveraged. Step 3: Net bilateral effect = CAD++ minus USD+ = net CAD positive = USD/CAD FALLS. Step 4: Quantify — Canada's energy sector is ~15% of GDP vs US ~9%; $10 change in WTI generates ~50–70 pips of USD/CAD movement (Canada captures more). Step 5: WCS-WTI compression from $15.75 to $12/bbl adds ~$3.75/bbl incremental Canadian revenue not captured in WTI headline. Step 6: TMX utilisation at 82% of 890K bbl/d confirms Pacific pricing capture. Net: oil at current levels ($81–83) is a clear USD/CAD negative (pair falls) because Canada's benefit asymmetrically exceeds the US benefit.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_up_10pct: Oil +10% (WTI ~$90): USD/CAD falls approximately 50–70 pips. Canada's energy revenues increase ~$17B annualised, current account improves further toward balance. WCS-WTI may compress further. TMX captures incremental Brent premium. USD safe-haven bid partially offsets but net is USD/CAD lower by ~50–70 pips.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: Oil -10% (WTI ~$73): USD/CAD rises approximately 40–60 pips. Canada's energy revenue advantage narrows. WCS-WTI may widen back toward $14–15/bbl as TMX demand softens. Alberta fiscal position deteriorates. BoC terms-of-trade assessment turns less favourable. Net: USD/CAD rises ~40–60 pips but structural TMX benefit partially buffers the move.
net_commodity_exposure.dominant_commodity_driver: WTI crude oil and WCS-WTI differential are the dominant commodity drivers for USD/CAD, with the bilateral asymmetry (Canada ~15% energy GDP vs US ~9%) meaning oil moves generate larger CAD moves than USD moves. The WCS-WTI spread is the pair-specific signal most unique to USD/CAD that no other G10 pair tracks.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The guidance comparison between the Fed and BoC at the tactical horizon creates a genuine deadlock. The Fed is data-dependent and meeting-by-meeting, offering no pre-commitment. The BoC moved to explicitly two-way guidance at its January 28 meeting ('difficult to predict timing and direction of the next change'), closing the easing chapter without opening a hiking chapter. Neither bank is providing tradeable forward guidance — both are in reactive mode. The March 18 simultaneous announcements create a 24-hour binary where the relative surprise determines USD/CAD direction. Three scenarios: (1) Dovish Fed (2+ dot cuts) + neutral BoC = USD/CAD falls 50–100 pips; (2) Hawkish Fed (0 dot cuts) + neutral BoC = USD/CAD rises 50–80 pips; (3) Both neutral = range consolidation. The credibility comparison favours neither: the Fed delivered all its projected cuts in 2025, but is now trapped in stagflation; the BoC also followed through on all nine cuts but has now reached the lower bound of neutral. Both banks have high institutional credibility, providing a neutral bilateral signal.

Fed last guidance: January 28, 2026 — 'data-dependent,' no commitment

tactical.what_is_priced: The market has priced neither bank as surprising — 94% hold probabilities for both. The dot plot outcome is the unpriced variable; the BoC language has minimal event risk given no MPR.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic guidance comparison creates a clear asymmetry favouring CAD. The BoC's two-way guidance has optionality: any shift toward hawkishness is a positive surprise for CAD (not priced), while a shift toward dovishness would merely confirm what bears expect. The Fed faces the opposite asymmetry: at 9/10 factors bearing, any hawkish shift would be a surprise relief (positive USD/CAD), while the path of least resistance is toward more cuts. The April 29 BoC MPR is the strategic inflection — a GDP forecast upgrade above 1.5% and acknowledgement that core inflation is plateauing at 2.4–2.5% would signal potential hike readiness. Meanwhile, the Fed Chair transition (Warsh from May 15) creates institutional uncertainty that is net-USD-negative through policy opacity. The credibility differential between a Warsh-led Fed and a stable Macklem-led BoC (term through June 2027) favours CAD on a relative institutional credibility basis.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The near-term event landscape is paradoxically CAD-positive despite broad risk-off. Three events dominate: (1) The Iran/Hormuz situation is uniquely beneficial for Canada among G10 nations because Canada's crude reaches Pacific markets via TMX without transit through the Strait of Hormuz. The WTI prompt spread backwardation of $14.20 (record) reflects acute supply tightness that flows directly to Canadian energy revenues. Trump's statement that the operation is 'nearing conclusion' introduces ceasefire risk — oil retracing would reduce the CAD tailwind but USD safe-haven premium would also fade, likely net-neutral to slightly CAD-positive (the safe-haven bid is more artificial than the commodity terms-of-trade improvement). (2) The March 16–19 super-week dominates: seven central bank decisions in three days. Key CAD crosses face major volatility risks as the Fed and BoC line up back-to-back hawkish holds; volatility is already elevated in USD/CAD, and both EUR/CAD and AUD/CAD are probing key technical levels. (3) US-Canada trade talks resumed March 6 — the first formal engagement since October 2025 termination. This is a modest CAD-positive signal.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic event landscape is dominated by two binary outcomes. First, Canada is negotiating bilateral agreements to resolve industrial tariffs alongside the mandatory 2026 USMCA review, aiming to secure long-term stability for steel, aluminum, and automotive sectors. Despite private discussions within the White House regarding potential US departure from the agreement, a broad consensus remains among business stakeholders regarding its value — over 75% of experts consulted by the US government support maintaining the USMCA, with only 2% expressing opposition. Second, OPEC+ is gradually restoring output (+206K bbl/d for April, June 7 ministerial is next decision) — if Iran resolves and OPEC+ accelerates, oil could retrace to $60–65, eliminating the CAD terms-of-trade tailwind. The bilateral trade relationship is deeply integrated, with bilateral trade of approximately $1.9 billion per day along the world's longest undefended border, with energy trade as the dominant component. The mutual dependency creates a built-in pressure for USMCA resolution.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The fiscal comparison between the US and Canada creates a clear bilateral differential that is USD-negative but slow-moving. Canada maintains AAA ratings from S&P and Moody's with federal debt-to-GDP at 41.2%, while the US has lost all AAA ratings (AA+/Aa1/AA+) with debt-to-GDP at 99.8% and a twin deficit of 8.8% of GDP. Canada's CGB market shows zero fiscal risk premium — spreads are driven by monetary policy. The US UST term premium (2s10s at +57–62bps) partially reflects fiscal supply concerns. Tactically, this differential is fully priced and generates no new signal.

The AAA differential creates structural demand for Canadian government bonds vs USTs, particularly from official sector investors diversifying away from USD. However, this is a slow-moving process that operates over years, not months.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The USD/CAD pair benefits from unique bilateral funding dynamics: Canada's deep integration with the US financial system means that global USD tightening impacts CAD less than it impacts AUD, NZD, or NOK. The standing Fed-BoC swap line provides a structural backstop that prevents acute USD funding squeezes from pressuring CAD disproportionately. The Fed has ended QT and commenced $40B/month reserve management purchases (RMPs), which adds dollar liquidity and reduces the structural scarcity premium. The CAD/USD cross-currency basis is estimated at -5 to -10bps — significantly tighter than the EUR/USD basis at -15 to -20bps, reflecting Canada's direct US financial integration. SOFR at 3.65% with zero spread to IORB indicates healthy funding. The dollar smile framework currently places USD on the left side (strong in crisis) due to the Iran war, but this is fading. Tactically, the USD liquidity picture is mildly supportive for USD/CAD given safe-haven demand, offset by the RMP injection trend.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic USD liquidity picture is one of the strongest bilateral signals for USD/CAD weakness. The Fed's pivot from QT to RMPs is mechanically increasing dollar liquidity, which narrows the USD scarcity premium that supported cross-currency demand for dollars over the past two years. Over 6 months at $40B/month, approximately $240B in reserve additions will flow through the system, keeping SOFR stable and narrowing basis swaps. For the USD/CAD pair specifically, the rate-convergence mechanism operates through this channel: as the Fed cuts, the CAD/USD basis remains tight (Canada's deep integration), but the rate differential compresses, reducing the incentive to hold USD vs CAD purely on yield. Canada's unique position as a US-integrated economy means this benefit flows more directly to CAD than to any other G10 non-USD currency.

tactical.rationale: The tactical picture for USD/CAD is a study in conflicting timeframes: the bilateral fundamentals lean bearish USD/CAD (Factors 2, 4, 5, 7 all negative) while the two most important factors — Policy & Rates (0.24 weight, neutral due to static carry) and Market Structure (0.07 tactical weight, bullish on RSI 20.1 mean-reversion) — provide offsetting signals. Factor 1's zero contribution is the critical insight: the -138bps carry differential is a mechanical tax on long-CAD positions that, combined with the deeply oversold technical condition, argues strongly against initiating new bearish USD/CAD positions at current levels. The bilateral dossier combination reveals that both standalone views are in the same directional direction (USD bearish standalone, CAD bullish standalone) but the bilateral carry cost and technical overshoot mean the PAIR signal is weaker than either standalone suggests. The March 16–19 super-week is the decisive resolution event: seven central bank decisions in three days will either confirm the breakout (below 1.3580) or trigger the technical bounce (toward 1.37). Options market confirms this with backwardated vol term structure and 1-week IV trading at a premium to 1-month. The risk reversals' mild put-bid (bearish USD/CAD) on both 1-week and 1-month tenors is a meaningful confirming signal from smart money. The Iran war paradox for this pair — Canada benefits more from oil at $81+ than the US does — means the geopolitical factor is working slightly counter to its usual risk-off USD-positive channel. Path of least resistance: technical bounce to 1.37 first (RSI normalisation), then super-week resolution determines whether this is a sell-the-rally or a genuine trend reversal. Conviction 2/5 supports existing short-USD/CAD positions with tighter stops but not new position initiation at current levels.

strategic.rationale: The strategic case for bearish USD/CAD rests on three mutually reinforcing structural pillars. The dominant driver is Policy & Rates convergence (-0.144 contribution): the Fed is expected to cut at least once to 3.375% by September while the BoC holds (or hikes toward 2.50–2.75% in the Scotiabank scenario), compressing the -138bps bilateral spread toward -100bps or narrower. This mechanically reduces the carry cost of holding long-CAD/short-USD — the single most important tactical headwind — and removes the primary rationale for leveraged fund CAD shorts. The External Balance factor (-0.111 contribution) provides the structural foundation: Canada stands out as the country with the largest net energy balance relative to the size of its economy, equivalent to 4.4% of nominal GDP, while the US runs an 8.8% of GDP twin deficit and a -$27.6T NIIP. The TMX-driven WCS-WTI compression is a secular shift that most bank models have not incorporated, creating a persistent underpricing of CAD fair value. USD Liquidity (-0.072 contribution) provides the transmission mechanism: Fed RMPs at $40B/month inject dollar liquidity over 6 months, compressing the USD funding premium and reducing the structural demand for dollars that has kept USD elevated. These three factors interact: rate convergence attracts capital away from USD; improving Canadian energy terms-of-trade generate current account improvement; dollar liquidity easing removes the scarcity premium. The bilateral interaction between the two dossiers is reinforcing, not conflicting: both standalone views point toward USD weakness/CAD strength, but the bilateral expression through USD/CAD is moderated by the carry cost (Factor 1 tactical weight) and USMCA risk (Factor 7). The key bilateral divergence from bank consensus is our more bearish USD/CAD target of 1.28–1.30 vs consensus 1.33–1.34 — driven by TMX/LNG Canada infrastructure that static bank models haven't fully incorporated. The path requires a constructive USMCA outcome (base case 55% probability) and oil above $70 (base case 65% probability at 6M horizon). USMCA renegotiations are a downside risk to CAD for H1, but we expect this to be temporary as the US-Canada economic linkages are a two-way street and the US faces midterm elections in November.

Among CAD crosses, the USD/CAD short (CAD long) competes with long AUD/CAD (RBA hawkishness, +160bps rate advantage) and long NOK/CAD (Norges Bank at 4.00%, +175bps over BoC). BoC forced to cut to 1.75–2.00% as recession materialises (back-to-back negative GDP quarters)
BoC hikes 25bps in Q3 (Scotiabank scenario), narrowing spread to -75bps
Fed cuts twice (June and September) and USMCA extension confirmed

strategic.path_expectation: The expected path unfolds in three phases. Phase 1 (March–April 2026): consolidation within 1.34–1.39 as the super-week resolves and the market awaits the April 29 BoC MPR. The pair is likely to trade without strong directional conviction during this phase as the Iran war premium fades and USMCA uncertainty builds toward July. Phase 2 (May–June): directional move lower in USD/CAD as Fed cut expectations solidify (June FOMC is potentially live for first cut at ~20% probability, September more likely at ~60%), and pre-USMCA positioning builds. The pair targets 1.33–1.35 by end of June. Phase 3 (July–August): binary USMCA resolution — constructive outcome drives a final leg toward 1.30–1.32; confrontational outcome reverses toward 1.40. Volatility follows an inverted U-shape: declining from current elevated levels through April, stabilising in May, then spiking into the July USMCA deadline. Pullbacks within the downtrend toward 1.38–1.39 should be used as entry points for strategic short positions.

increase_conditions: BoC April MPR raises 2026 GDP forecast above 1.5% AND Fed June meeting signals September cut firmly, OR USMCA pre-review signals constructive extension — increase to 4/5
BoC hikes 25bps in Q3 AND USMCA extension confirmed simultaneously — increase to 5/5
decrease_conditions: Q1 2026 GDP contracts (technical recession) OR USMCA review signals confrontational posture (annual reviews) — decrease to 2/5
BoC cuts to 2.00% AND oil falls below $60/bbl AND USMCA non-extension signalled simultaneously — decrease to 1/5

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Moderately favourable. The aggressive downside scenario (BoC hike + USMCA extension + dual Fed cuts) offers +900 pips vs the upside scenario loss of -400 pips. The wide positive asymmetry justifies Conviction 3 and supports size building on bounces.

consensus_divergence.bank_consensus_forecast: Aggregate bank consensus clusters around USD/CAD 1.33–1.34 by Q4 2026. RBC: 1.3400 by end-2026 (conditional on BoC hikes and duty-free exports). Scotiabank: 1.30 by end-2026 (most bullish CAD). ING: 1.34. Capital.com aggregate: USD/CAD declining from 1.40 in 2025 to 1.30 by 2027.
consensus_divergence.our_tactical_view: USD/CAD range-bound 1.345–1.385 over 2–4 weeks; lean bearish but not actionable at current RSI 20.1 levels. Wait for bounce to 1.37–1.38 before initiating short.
consensus_divergence.our_strategic_view: USD/CAD 1.30–1.33 by August 2026 — approximately 3–5% more bearish USD/CAD than median bank consensus.
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: ~300–400 pips below median bank consensus at 6M horizon
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: Banks are systematically underweighting Canada's structural energy export infrastructure improvement. The combination of TMX Pacific access (permanent WCS-WTI compression of ~$3–5/bbl) and LNG Canada Phase 1 ramping to 14 MTPA creates a secular improvement in Canada's terms of trade that static bank FX models — calibrated before TMX operations — have not incorporated. Additionally, consensus treats USMCA as a tail risk rather than pricing the constructive extension scenario (55–60% probability) as a positive catalyst that would remove 3–5% risk premium currently embedded in USD/CAD. We also assign higher weight to the Fed-BoC convergence dynamic than the consensus median because the Scotiabank hike scenario (underweighted by consensus) could be triggered by the April MPR. Most bank models use pre-TMX calibration periods and static oil assumptions, causing them to systematically underestimate Canada's structural current account improvement.
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: Consensus is correct at 1.33–1.34 — still profit 170–270 pips vs consensus. The real downside risk is the USMCA confrontation + recession scenario at 1.40–1.43 = 430–730 pip loss.

cross_horizon_consistency.explanation: The tactical and strategic stances are directionally aligned (both bearish USD/CAD) but diverge on conviction and timing (tactical at 2/5, strategic at 3/5). This is a consistent, not contradictory, cross-horizon view. The divergence is mechanistically explained by three weight-structure differences: (1) Policy & Rates contributes zero at the tactical horizon (static carry, no catalyst) but -0.144 at strategic (rate convergence is a 3–6 month process); (2) External Balance has 0.08 tactical weight vs 0.17 strategic — the TMX/energy infrastructure story is structural, not tactical; (3) Market Structure is weighted 0.07 tactically (mean-reversion risk from RSI 20.1) and 0.03 strategically (fades as vol normalises).
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: March 18–19, 2026 (FOMC + BoC 24-hour window). A dovish dot plot aligned with a neutral-hawkish BoC would align both horizons at Conviction 3 bearish USD/CAD.
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Do not initiate new short USD/CAD positions at current levels (1.3570, RSI 20.1). Wait for: (a) technical bounce to 1.37–1.38 area (50-day SMA), or (b) confirmed directional break below 1.3580 post-super-week, then build the strategic position with a stop at 1.3828 (200-day SMA).

forward_vs_spot.edge_3m: +174 pips (forward too high vs our expected rate; forward implies 1.3604, we expect 1.3430)
forward_vs_spot.edge_6m: +488 pips (forward too high vs our expected rate; forward implies 1.3638, we expect 1.3150)

catalysts.rationale: February CPI directly influences the March FOMC dot plot composition. A hot print (core +0.3% MoM) reinforces the stagflation narrative and reduces probability of Fed cuts in 2026, widening the -138bps bilateral spread marginally and pushing USD/CAD higher by approximately 30–50 pips per standard deviation surprise. A cool print accelerates the June cut repricing, compressing the spread and pushing USD/CAD lower by a similar magnitude. The bilateral transmission runs through the rate differential channel: higher US CPI → Fed constrained → carry differential widens → leveraged fund CAD shorts add → USD/CAD rises.

A spike in crude oil prices had traders piling into the Canadian dollar, and the February CPI spike toward ~2.9% driven by gasoline is expected. If February CPI prints at or above 3.0% — possible given the oil surge — the BoC would face explicit stagflationary pressure, likely triggering market repricing of BoC hike probability. This is paradoxically CAD-positive: higher Canadian inflation → BoC tightening repricing → bilateral spread compression from the CAD side → USD/CAD falls. The magnitude could be 30–60 pips if CPI prints above 3.0%. The BoC receives this data the day before its March 18 statement, making it directly influential on the statement's tone.

The single highest-impact event for USD/CAD in the tactical horizon. Updated staff projections will be released; Bank of America estimates two 25bp cuts while Goldman Sachs anticipates just one. If median dot shifts to 2 cuts (dovish surprise), USD/CAD falls 50–80 pips; 0 cuts (hawkish surprise), USD/CAD rallies 50–80 pips. The bilateral transmission runs through the carry channel: Fed dot compression → BoC-Fed spread narrows → carry cost of long-CAD falls → leveraged fund shorts cover → USD/CAD accelerates lower. The base case (1 cut median) is roughly priced and would be neutral to mildly USD/CAD-negative.

94% probability of hold makes the rate decision itself a non-event. The statement language is the variable — any shift from two-way guidance to a hawkish lean on February CPI would be CAD-positive by 30–50 pips. The BoC meets the day after the FOMC, meaning it will receive the Fed dot plot before finalising its language — a unique setting where the BoC could respond to Fed dovishness with a hawkish lean, compressing the spread from both sides simultaneously. A combined dovish-FOMC + hawkish-BoC outcome would be the most powerful bilateral CAD-positive catalyst available in the near-term.

catalysts.event: ECB, BoJ, BoE, SNB, Norges Bank Decisions (Five in One Day)
catalysts.rationale: The five concurrent decisions on March 19 create cross-rate volatility that spills into USD/CAD through the vehicle currency. BoJ hold supports USD/JPY stability, which reduces safe-haven USD demand and is indirectly bearish USD/CAD. BoE decision (50/50 cut) affects GBP/USD and USD generally. SNB hold is CAD-neutral. The cumulative spillover from five simultaneous decisions creates position unwinding pressure that has historically generated 20–40 pips of additional USD/CAD volatility beyond direct event transmission. The dominant risk is a surprise BoE cut combined with BoJ hold — moderately CAD-positive through USD weakness.

Flash January GDP estimate was 0.0% (flat). A confirmed negative January GDP alongside the Q4 -0.6% contraction would signal two consecutive monthly declines, potentially triggering OECD/RBC recession flags. The market is pricing 0.0%; a negative revision reopens the BoC cut debate and would widen the bilateral spread by effectively raising the probability of a BoC cut before year-end. The bilateral mechanism: negative Canada GDP → BoC cut probability rises → spread widens from -138bps toward -163bps → carry trade re-asserts → USD/CAD rises 30–60 pips.

catalysts.event: Bank of Canada Rate Decision + Monetary Policy Report
catalysts.rationale: The most important BoC event in the strategic horizon. The April MPR updates the 2026 GDP forecast (currently 1.1%) and the full inflation outlook. If the BoC upgrades GDP above 1.5% and flags upside inflation risks, the market would begin pricing BoC hike probability, accelerating USD/CAD downside toward 1.34–1.35 quickly. This is the event that converts the Scotiabank hike scenario from outlier to mainstream and the primary trigger for conviction upgrading from 3/5 to 4/5. The bilateral mechanism: BoC hawkish MPR → hike probability priced → spread compresses toward -100bps or below → leveraged fund shorts forced to cover → USD/CAD falls 80–120 pips.

catalysts.event: Fed Chair Powell Term Expiration / Warsh Transition
catalysts.rationale: Kevin Warsh's succession creates communication uncertainty. Markets overwhelmingly agree that whoever replaces Powell will be more friendly to the idea of faster interest rate cuts. However, the transition itself creates policy opacity — markets dislike central bank leadership changes. For USD/CAD, institutional USD uncertainty is net-bearish for USD: the uncertainty premium should compress the pair by 50–100 pips over the transition period. The contrast with BoC Governor Macklem (term through June 2027, stable and known) creates a relative credibility differential that favours CAD. Paradoxically, Warsh's perceived dovishness is a longer-term positive for CAD by accelerating Fed cuts, while the immediate transition uncertainty is negative for USD.

OPEC+ is gradually unwinding voluntary cuts (+206K bbl/d for April). If the June ministerial accelerates to +400K bbl/d+, oil supply expectations shift bearish and WTI could retrace to $65–70, partially reversing the Iranian war premium. This would be the primary strategic risk for the CAD terms-of-trade thesis, as TMX/WCS benefits diminish at lower oil prices (though the structural spread compression persists). The bilateral mechanism: OPEC+ acceleration → WTI falls → WCS-WTI may widen back toward $15+ → Canada current account deteriorates → BoC terms-of-trade assessment turns less favourable → USD/CAD rises 50–80 pips.

catalysts.event: FOMC Decision + SEP (Most Likely First Cut Meeting)
catalysts.rationale: June FOMC is likely the first meeting where a cut is live (~20% probability by market pricing) or September is firmly confirmed (~60%). A June cut would immediately compress the BoC-Fed spread from -138bps toward -113bps, the first actual narrowing in the bilateral rate differential. This is the most powerful single catalyst for USD/CAD downside in the strategic horizon. The bilateral mechanism: Fed cuts → rate differential compresses -25bps → carry cost of long-CAD falls from 47 to 39 pips per quarter → leveraged fund covering → asset managers adding → USD/CAD targets 1.33–1.34 in the near term.

The USMCA's six-year review clause requires parties to formally assess the agreement's performance and agree to extend it. If they do not, the agreement enters a rolling annual review cycle. Legally the review is not a renegotiation; politically however it has become one. A constructive start removes 3–5% risk premium from USD/CAD (pair falls 400–700 pips); a confrontational start adds 3–5% (pair rises 400–700 pips). Despite discussions regarding potential US departure, over 75% of experts consulted by the US government support maintaining the USMCA, with only 2% expressing opposition. This is the binary event that determines whether the 10–12% USD/CAD premium above PPP is fully unwound or expands further.

catalysts.event: Bank of Canada Rate Decision + MPR (Potentially Live Meeting for Hike)
catalysts.rationale: If April MPR signalled hawkish lean and Q2 data confirms growth stabilisation with sticky inflation, July becomes a live meeting for a 25bp hike to 2.50%. This is the Scotiabank base case and would narrow the BoC-Fed spread from -138bps toward -100bps instantaneously upon announcement, generating 100–150 pips of USD/CAD downside. The bilateral mechanism operates through two channels: (1) direct rate differential compression narrows carry cost; (2) psychological shock to leveraged fund shorts who have been positioned on the basis of BoC on hold — a hike forces rapid covering. This event is the single most impactful potential CAD catalyst in the strategic horizon and remains significantly underpriced at <15% probability.

invalidators.condition: USMCA Free Trade Commission signals annual reviews (non-extension) or US threatens to exit the agreement in July 2026
invalidators.mechanism: The 71.7% of Canadian exports exposed to the US would face existential trade architecture risk, adding 3–5% structural risk premium to USD/CAD. Canada's government would face significant trade disruption as the broader suspension would signal a significant shift in bilateral trade relations, forcing asset managers to unwind structural CAD longs and potentially adding 400–700 pips to USD/CAD.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: USMCA Free Trade Commission press releases, USTR communications, White House statements on Canada trade

invalidators.condition: BoC cuts rates to 2.00% at any meeting through September 2026
invalidators.mechanism: A BoC cut would widen the bilateral spread from -138bps toward -163bps; confirm Canada is in or approaching recession, triggering risk-off selling of CAD by leveraged funds; and cause asset manager CAD longs to begin unwinding, reversing the structural buying flow. The combination of spread widening and positioning reversal could generate 200–300 pips of USD/CAD upside.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Statistics Canada quarterly GDP releases and BoC statements
invalidators.monitor_frequency: Monthly GDP data, 8x annual BoC decisions

invalidators.condition: WTI crude oil falls and sustains below $60/bbl for 14+ calendar days
invalidators.mechanism: Canada's terms-of-trade improvement evaporates at WTI below $60: the current account deteriorates toward -2.0% of GDP, Alberta faces fiscal crisis (budget assumes $60.50 WTI), and the primary bilateral differentiation factor (Canada's energy export advantage) is neutralised. The WCS-WTI spread would likely widen back toward $15–16/bbl, eliminating the TMX structural benefit. USD/CAD would likely rise 150–200 pips from current levels.

invalidators.condition: Q1 2026 GDP annualised prints negative following Q4 2025 -0.6% contraction (back-to-back quarterly contractions = technical recession)
invalidators.mechanism: Back-to-back quarterly contractions constitute a technical recession, forcing the BoC to abandon its two-way guidance and consider emergency easing, collapsing the rate convergence thesis. Asset managers holding structural CAD longs would begin reversing, and leveraged fund shorts would increase their positions. The bilateral spread would likely widen from -138bps toward -163bps or more. USD/CAD could rise 100–150 pips rapidly on the data release.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Statistics Canada GDP release (May 2026 for Q1 data)

invalidators.condition: USD/CAD closes above 200-day SMA at 1.3828 for three consecutive sessions
invalidators.mechanism: The structural downtrend from 1.4791 is technically broken; systematic/CTA flows flip from short to long USD/CAD, creating a self-reinforcing rally. Leveraged funds add new shorts well below break-even levels, further compressing positioning. The 200-day SMA break would signal the five-month downtrend has run its course and invite new trend-following longs, potentially targeting 1.40–1.43.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: USD/CAD daily closing prices vs 200-day SMA at 1.3828

invalidators.condition: Strait of Hormuz fully closed for 14+ calendar days due to Iran war escalation
invalidators.mechanism: While Canada's TMX provides non-Hormuz Pacific routing, a full Hormuz closure for 14+ days would push WTI above $120, triggering global demand destruction and recession fears that ultimately hurt all risk currencies including CAD. The safe-haven USD bid would overwhelm the oil tailwind as global growth collapses. USD/CAD would likely rise despite oil at extreme levels because the recession signal dominates the terms-of-trade signal.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: WTI above $130 sustained plus tanker traffic data through the Strait of Hormuz

signposts.confirms_if: Sustains above $70/bbl through Q2 2026 — Canada's energy revenues secure and terms-of-trade improvement intact
signposts.invalidates_if: Falls below $60/bbl for 14+ days — eliminates CAD terms-of-trade tailwind and triggers Alberta fiscal stress
signposts.bilateral_importance: Oil matters MORE for USD/CAD than for most G10 pairs because Canada is a net oil exporter while the US is approximately break-even, creating a clear bilateral asymmetry. A $10 change in WTI generates approximately 50–70 pips of USD/CAD movement. No other G10 bilateral pair has this same structural oil asymmetry.

signposts.confirms_if: Narrows toward -70bps (Fed cut priced, BoC convergence underway)
signposts.invalidates_if: Widens to -120bps or more (BoC cuts while Fed holds, spreading the bilateral differential)
signposts.bilateral_importance: The 2Y spread is the most direct real-time indicator of the BoC-Fed policy divergence that drives the entire bilateral thesis. A narrowing spread reduces the carry cost of long-CAD — the single biggest impediment to the bullish CAD view. Every 10bps narrowing reduces the quarterly carry bleed by approximately 3–4 pips.

signposts.confirms_if: Remains below $14/bbl — TMX Pacific access sustaining structural compression and Canada capturing Brent-linked premium
signposts.invalidates_if: Widens above $18/bbl — pipeline apportionment re-emerging or TMX underperforming capacity
signposts.bilateral_importance: Unique to USD/CAD — the WCS-WTI spread captures the structural infrastructure improvement from TMX operations that most bank models miss. Compression below $14 confirms Canada is capturing incremental revenue vs pre-TMX baseline. This signpost is not directly relevant to any other G10 pair and represents the pair-specific differentiation factor in our analysis.

signposts.indicator: CFTC Leveraged Fund Net CAD Position
signposts.confirms_if: Short-covering toward flat (0 to -20K contracts) — leveraged funds capitulating on carry trade and validating fundamental bullish CAD thesis
signposts.invalidates_if: Shorts build beyond -70K contracts — carry trade dominant and CAD bears firmly in control with fresh commitment
signposts.bilateral_importance: For USD/CAD, the leveraged fund position is the single best indicator of the carry trade's health. The -138bps carry cost attracts short-CAD positions; covering them is the mechanical fuel for USD/CAD downside moves. More directly relevant to this pair than any other because the carry cost differential is uniquely large, making leveraged fund positioning particularly reactive to rate differential changes.

signposts.confirms_if: Remains in 2.3–2.7% range for 2+ consecutive months — stable inflation allows BoC to maintain current stance and supports eventual hawkish pivot at April MPR
signposts.invalidates_if: Both CPI-trim and CPI-median fall below 2.0% for two consecutive months — deflation risk reopens BoC cut debate and widens bilateral spread
signposts.bilateral_importance: BoC inflation matters for USD/CAD specifically because the BoC at 2.25% is at the lower bound of neutral — any signal that inflation is returning to or falling below target raises probability of further BoC cuts that widen the bilateral spread from the CAD side. The current 2.4% level is crucial for maintaining the BoC's two-way guidance, which is the foundation of the rate convergence thesis.

signposts.confirms_if: Spot remains below and SMA continues to slope downward — structural downtrend intact and technical trend-followers aligned with fundamental bearish view
signposts.invalidates_if: Spot closes above for 3 consecutive sessions — structural downtrend broken and CTA flows flip to long USD/CAD
signposts.bilateral_importance: This specific level is the most-watched technical invalidation threshold in USD/CAD — a break above triggers systematic buying from CTAs and trend-following funds that would create self-reinforcing momentum regardless of fundamentals. The distance from current spot (258 pips) provides a quantifiable stop level for all strategic short positions.

assumptions: Spot reference: USD/CAD 1.3570 as of March 11, 2026. Bid-offer spread of approximately 1–2 pips for major execution; wider in event windows.
Forward points estimated from interest rate parity using BoC (2.25%) vs Fed (3.625%) rates, adjusted for basis swap estimates. Live quotes may differ by 5–15 pips at execution.

Implied volatility: 1M USD/CAD IV estimated at ~6.5%, 3M at ~5.8%, based on the Iran/super-week event premium and historical vol-oil correlations. Terminal data required for precision; these estimates carry ±0.5% error bands.
Risk reversals: The direction (puts bid = bearish USD/CAD) is confirmed by multiple sources; the magnitude (-0.3 to -0.5 vols) is estimated. Live screen required for precision.

CFTC positioning: Data as of March 3, 2026 (released March 7). The Iran oil shock and subsequent volatility between March 3–11 means current positioning may have shifted by 5,000–10,000 contracts in either direction.
PPP deviation: OECD-methodology based estimate of 10–12% CAD undervaluation vs USD. Consistent across OECD and Big Mac Index approaches but not a tactical timing tool.

Bank consensus: RBC 1.34, Scotiabank 1.30 by end-2026 (most bullish CAD), ING 1.34. The consensus range is 1.30–1.36 with a mode at 1.33–1.34. Our 1.28–1.30 target is at the bearish extreme of the distribution.

USMCA probability: Base case 55% constructive extension, 20% confrontational/annual reviews, 25% uncertain interim (bilateral sectoral deals replacing full extension). These are judgment-based estimates given the binary nature of the outcome.

Weight adjustments: External Balance was increased from 0.06 to 0.08 tactically and 0.16 to 0.17 strategically to reflect Canada's unique structural energy export advantage (TMX/LNG) not captured in the default framework calibrated for more generic commodity-currency pairs.
